title: NR7 + Opening Range Breakout: edge does not survive trading costs (42 US futures, 1980-2016)
claim: Crabel's NR7 setup traded via opening-range-breakout stretch entry, tested across 42 US futures 1980-2016, is not tradeable after transaction costs without additional filter rules; the pattern works better with NR_Length >= 6 and longer holding periods.

source.label: Oxford Capital Strategies — NR7 Pattern | Trading Strategy (Setup & Exit)
source.quote: The current daily range is narrower than the previous six days' daily ranges compared individually ... A buy stop is placed at [Open + Stretch] ... The NR7 pattern performs better when NR_Length >= 6 ... Once the cost of trading is applied, the pattern is not currently tradeable without some additional rules

rule_formal: NR7: Range(t) < min(Range(t-1..t-6)). Entry next day: buy stop = Open + Stretch, sell stop = Open - Stretch, Stretch = avg(10-day noise) * 2.0. Exits tested: (a) close on Nth day, (b) opposite stretch level, (c) stop = Entry -/+ ATR(20) * 6.
mechanism: Range contraction compresses positioning; the first directional move from the open forces breakout entries in one direction — but per this test the per-trade edge is smaller than realistic costs.
relevance: Q1 (breakout entry off a compression day = same family as Asian-range breakout; negative cost result is a prior against adding NR-style day filters expecting large edge), Q2 (NR7 as day-before trend-day antecedent)

test_design.lookahead_risk: none — NR7 known at prior day close; stretch computable at day open

overlap_risk: high overlap with rv percentile and asian width ratio (both are range-contraction measures already falsified on this system)
